perturbation.original_text: At any time upon the Compan y’s written request (including by email), Buye r agrees that Buyer and its Representatives shall promptly destroy or erase all Evaluation Material (including any Evaluation Material held electronically) in the possession or control of Buyer or any of its Representatives, and Buyer shall, if requested in writing (including by email) by the Company, certify such destruction or erasure to the Company
perturbation.changed_text: At any time upon the Company’s written request (including by email), Buyer agrees that Buyer and its Representatives shall attempt to destroy or erase Evaluation Material in their direct possession. Buyer may, if possible, certify such destruction or erasure to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall promptly destroy or erase all Evaluation Material' with 'shall attempt to destroy or erase Evaluation Material in their direct possession' weakens the obligation to destroy all confidential information, and limiting it to direct possession excludes information held indirectly. Replacing 'Buyer shall certify such destruction or erasure to the Company' with 'Buyer may, if possible, certify such destruction or erasure to the Company' makes certification optional.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Business Law § 899-AA mandates specific procedures for the destruction of private information to prevent security breaches. By changing the obligation to 'attempt to destroy' and making certification 'if possible,' the modified text introduces ambiguity that could result in non-compliance with the law's requirements for verified and complete destruction of such information. The limitation to direct possession also could run afoul of the requirement to destroy 'all' private information.
perturbation.location: 5. Destruction or Erasure of Evaluation Material
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the buyer 'shall promptly destroy or erase all Evaluation Material,' while the changed version states they 'shall attempt to destroy or erase Evaluation Material in their direct possession.' This weakens the obligation and limits its scope. The original text also requires the buyer to 'certify such destruction or erasure to the Company,' whereas the modified text makes certification optional ('Buyer may, if possible, certify'). The Law Explanation suggests this introduces ambiguity that could result in non-compliance with New York General Business Law § 899-AA, which mandates specific procedures for the destruction of private information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to NYS General Business Law Article 39-F regarding data security protections, indicating a legal concern about data handling practices. The modified contract weakens the requirement to destroy data and verify destruction, creating a contradiction with the intent of data protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees that all communications by it or any of its Representatives with the ot her Party concerning the Transaction and its due diligence investigation (including requests for additional Evaluation Material, meetings with management and site visits) shall be directed solely to [___________________] , except as may otherwise be approved in advance and in writing (including by email) by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees that communications by it or any of its Representatives with the other Party concerning the Transaction should generally be directed to [___________________] , unless it seems more efficient to do otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall be directed solely to' with 'should generally be directed to' and adding 'unless it seems more efficient to do otherwise' introduces ambiguity and provides an easy loophole to circumvent the intended communication protocol.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Rules of Professional Conduct, Rule 4.2
perturbation.law_citation: NY Rules of Prof. Conduct, Rule 4.2
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Rules of Professional Conduct, Rule 4.2 (Communication with Person Represented by Counsel) prohibits a lawyer from communicating about the subject of the representation with a person the lawyer knows to be represented by another lawyer in the matter, unless the lawyer has the consent of the other lawyer or is authorized to do so by law or a court order. By allowing Buyers to bypass the designated contact person 'if it seems more efficient', this could result in direct communication with represented parties without proper consent, violating Rule 4.2.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: legal ethics | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you legal ethics Overview Legal ethics broadly refer to the unique responsibilities of lawyers and the legal system given the important role and influence they have in society. Because of their role and their close involvement in the administration of law, lawyers are subject to special standards, regulation, and liability. Most commonly, legal ethics refers to these rules of professional responsibility : the actual responsibilities lawyers must follow by law such as client confidentiality. However, legal ethics can also refer to the discussion on broader moral principles that societies place on lawyers that may not be legally required. Professional Responsibility The law governing the professional responsibility of lawyers, since they are admitted to practice by states, is largely governed by the law of each state. However, all states follow more or less the Model Rules of Professional Conduct created by the ABA , and federal courts and agencies set their own practice rules and apply specific rules like those in the Sarbanes-Oxley Act . While the laws of each state actually govern, the Model Rules and their comments greatly influence the decisions of courts applying professional responsibility laws. The Model Rules set mandatory and discretionary rules and principles on a wide variety of lawyer practice including: client confidentiality , conflicts of interest , fiduciary responsibilities , commingling , and general malpractice . Should a lawyer breach these rules, state courts and enforcement agencies have wide discretion to punish the lawyer and breaching some of the rules like conflicts of interest can impact the outcome of the lawyer’s matters. Broader Legal Ethics Legal ethics can refer to a higher set of norms that lawyers should follow that may not be required by set legal rules. Many of the model rules try to include some of these broader principles. However, the model legal rules tend to be considered a floor of expected requirements of lawyers. Legal ethics can refer to the broader principles lawyers should strive for to ensure the protection and adequate representation of clients. While codes try their best to encourage ethical conduct of lawyers, the legal profession for centuries has struggled with balancing client protections with the often conflicting interests of lawyers and other clients. Thus, legal ethics often involves discussion of the ideals of lawyer conduct, irrespective of the mandates of legal rules. Further Resources For more information on the legal ethics rules and debates, see the Model Rules for Professional Conduct , this ABA Guide to State Ethics Resources , this Duke Law Guide to Legal Ethics Requirements , and this Notre Dame Journal of Law, Ethics, & Public Policy Article on the differences between and limitations of legal codes and ethical principles. [Last reviewed in March of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal education and writing legal history THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics wex articles legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'shall be directed solely to' with 'should generally be directed to' and adds 'unless it seems more efficient to do otherwise,' which introduces ambiguity and provides a loophole. The Law Explanation states that this could result in direct communication with represented parties without proper consent, violating Rule 4.2 of the New York Rules of Professional Conduct (Communication with Person Represented by Counsel). While the scraped snippets don't directly reference Rule 4.2, they confirm the existence of legal ethics rules governing communication, stating that lawyers are subject to special standards, regulation, and liability due to their role and influence in society and the legal system.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for a breach or a threatened breach of this Agreement and that each party shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief, without the posting of a bond or other security, as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach, in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that money damages might not be a sufficient remedy for a breach of this Agreement and that each party may seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief, with the understanding that the court will decide on the need of posting a bond or other security, as a remedy for any such breach, in addition to other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'would not be a sufficient remedy' to 'might not be a sufficient remedy' and 'shall be entitled to specific performance' to 'may seek specific performance' weakens the assurance of equitable relief. Further, the removal of 'without the posting of a bond or other security' makes it more difficult to obtain such relief.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules, Section 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules, Section 6301 outlines the grounds for preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders, but it also implies the court's discretion to require a bond. By weakening the language ensuring specific performance as a remedy and implying the court will determine posting bond (the original language precluded posting bond), the changed text shifts the balance away from guaranteed equitable relief for breach of contract, making enforcement less certain and more discretionary by the Court.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The original contract language provides a strong assurance of equitable relief (specific performance and injunctive relief) for a breach, explicitly waiving the requirement of posting a bond. The changed text weakens this assurance by changing 'would not be a sufficient remedy' to 'might not be a sufficient remedy' and 'shall be entitled to specific performance' to 'may seek specific performance.' Further, it removes the explicit waiver of the bond requirement, making the availability of equitable relief less certain and subject to the court's discretion. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not available to support the legal contradiction, the Law Explanation details how the change contradicts Section 6301 of the New York Civil Practice Law and Rules regarding preliminary injunctions. The change introduces an ambiguity regarding the assurance of equitable relief, making it more difficult to enforce the specific performance provision. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists, falling under the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition.
